metadata.summary: Swap file setup, vm.swappiness tuning, OOM killer priority (oom_score_adj, OOMScoreAdjust), and systemd cgroup memory limits (MemoryMax, MemoryHigh) for VPS servers running multi-service AI platforms.

content.title: Swap and Memory Management
content.summary: Configure swap files, tune vm.swappiness, set OOM killer priorities, and apply systemd cgroup memory limits to prevent unbounded growth and ensure predictable failure modes on memory-constrained servers.

why.p: Default swappiness=60 causes aggressive paging even when 20GB RAM is free, creating latency spikes during LLM API responses. Without MemoryMax in systemd services, a Celery worker processing a large context window can grow unbounded and trigger the kernel OOM killer — which then kills a random process, often the wrong one. Explicit OOMScoreAdjust values ensure that if OOM must kill something, it kills the least critical service first.

list.item: New server setup — create swap file and tune swappiness before deploying services
OOM kills appearing in dmesg/journalctl — set MemoryMax and OOMScoreAdjust per service
Server swapping despite available RAM — reduce swappiness
Planning memory allocation for a multi-service platform
Celery workers or LLM services have unbounded memory growth
Managed Kubernetes (memory limits belong in Pod specs, not host sysctl)
Servers with only 1-2GB RAM where swap is a primary resource (different sizing rules apply)
When Docker manages the services — set mem_limit in compose instead of systemd MemoryMax

rule.statement: For a server with 16–32 GB RAM, create a 4 GB swap file. Swap is a safety net — it prevents OOM kills under burst conditions but should rarely be used during normal operation.
rule.rationale: Large swap on a RAM-rich server does not improve performance. With swappiness=10, the kernel will only start using swap when RAM is nearly exhausted, so 4 GB covers burst situations without wasting disk on idle swap space.

example.title: Swap sizing guidelines
example.code: RAM Swap 1–2 GB → 2–4 GB (2× RAM; small VPS needs breathing room) 4–8 GB → 4–8 GB (equal to RAM) 16–32 GB → 4 GB (safety net only) 32+ GB → 1–2 GB (minimal; mainly for hibernation)

rule.statement: Use fallocate to create the swap file; set chmod 600 before mkswap; add to /etc/fstab; verify with findmnt --verify to guard against a typo that prevents boot.
rule.rationale: A swap file with world-readable permissions leaks RAM content to unprivileged users. An invalid /etc/fstab entry can render the server unbootable. findmnt --verify catches syntax errors before reboot.

example.title: Creating and persisting a swap file
example.code:
sudo fallocate -l 4G /swapfile sudo chmod 600 /swapfile sudo mkswap /swapfile sudo swapon /swapfile
# Persist across reboots echo '/swapfile none swap sw 0 0' | sudo tee -a /etc/fstab
# Verify fstab (broken entry can prevent boot) sudo findmnt --verify
# Confirm swap is active swapon --show free -h

rule.statement: Set vm.swappiness = 10 on servers with 8 GB+ RAM. Never set it to 0 — value 0 means the kernel will not swap at all and may OOM-kill instead of using the swap safety net.
rule.rationale: Default swappiness=60 causes the kernel to start swapping when 40% of RAM is used, creating latency spikes during LLM API calls. Value 10 delays swapping until RAM is almost full while still keeping the swap safety valve functional.

example.title: Setting persistent swappiness
example.code:
# Persist via sysctl.d echo 'vm.swappiness=10' | sudo tee /etc/sysctl.d/60-memory.conf sudo sysctl vm.swappiness=10 # apply immediately without reboot
# Verify sysctl vm.swappiness # vm.swappiness = 10

antipattern.reason: With swappiness=0 the kernel will never use swap and will OOM-kill processes instead. A value of 10 keeps the safety valve open.

rule.statement: Pair swappiness tuning with vm.dirty_ratio=15, vm.dirty_background_ratio=5, and vm.vfs_cache_pressure=50 for stable I/O behavior and longer inode/dentry cache retention.
rule.rationale: dirty_background_ratio=5 starts background writeback early, preventing a sudden I/O spike when dirty_ratio=15 is reached. vfs_cache_pressure=50 tells the kernel to reclaim page cache before dentry/inode caches, keeping filesystem metadata resident.

example.title: Comprehensive memory tuning in sysctl.d
example.code: # /etc/sysctl.d/60-memory.conf vm.swappiness = 10 vm.dirty_ratio = 15 vm.dirty_background_ratio = 5 vm.vfs_cache_pressure = 50 vm.max_map_count = 1048576 vm.overcommit_memory = 0

rule.statement: Set OOMScoreAdjust in systemd unit files to control kill order. Databases: −900. Core API: −300. Celery workers: 0. Static file servers: +100. Never set −1000 for application services — reserve that for kernel threads.
rule.rationale: Without explicit OOM priorities the kernel picks victims by memory usage alone, often killing the database instead of a worker. Explicit priorities ensure that if OOM must kill something, it kills the least critical service first, in a deterministic order.

example.title: OOM scoring priority table
example.code: Service OOMScoreAdjust Rationale PostgreSQL -900 Data integrity paramount Redis -700 Losing context cache is expensive RabbitMQ -600 Message broker must stay up nero-channel-web -300 User-facing API gateway nero-channel-tg -200 Telegram bridge nero-core (Celery) 0 Workers can be restarted nero-web +100 Static server, instant restart

example.title: Setting OOMScoreAdjust in a service file
example.code: # In the [Service] section of any .service file OOMScoreAdjust=-300

rule.statement: Set MemoryMax (hard kill limit) and MemoryHigh (soft throttle limit) on every service. Without limits, a Celery worker processing a large context window grows unbounded and triggers the kernel OOM killer against the wrong process.
rule.rationale: MemoryHigh causes the kernel to throttle the cgroup and activate memory reclaim before hitting MemoryMax. This gives the service a chance to release memory gracefully. MemoryMax is the hard backstop — the process receives SIGKILL when it exceeds this.

example.title: Recommended Memory Limits for NERO Platform
example.code: Service MemoryMax MemoryHigh Notes nero-core (Celery) 8G 6G LLM responses can be large nero-channel-web 2G 1500M FastAPI + connection pool nero-channel-tg 1G 768M Lightweight bot bridge nero-web (static) 512M 384M nginx-like static serving PostgreSQL 8G 6G shared_buffers + work_mem Redis 4G 3G context cache RabbitMQ 2G 1500M message queues

rule.statement: Monitor cgroup memory usage via systemctl status and the cgroup fs at /sys/fs/cgroup/. Use systemd-cgtop for a live overview of all services.
rule.rationale: High-water-mark memory usage is not visible in free -h — it is scoped to the cgroup. Checking memory.peak (if available) or watching memory.current reveals whether a service is regularly approaching its MemoryHigh limit and needs an increase.

example.title: Monitoring cgroup memory usage
example.code:
# Live overview of all user cgroups by memory systemd-cgtop
# Current memory for a specific service (systemd user services) UID=$(id -u) CGROUP="/sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-${UID}.slice/user@${UID}.service/nero-core.service" cat "$CGROUP/memory.current" # bytes currently used cat "$CGROUP/memory.max" # hard limit
# Per-process memory sorted by RSS ps aux --sort=-%mem | head -15
# Swap usage by process for f in /proc/[0-9]*/status; do awk '/VmSwap|Name/{printf $2 " " $3}END{print ""}' "$f" done | sort -k2 -rn | head -10

template.purpose: Idempotent swap file creation and fstab persistence
/etc/sysctl.d/ drop-in for swappiness and dirty page tuning
systemd service memory limit and OOM policy directives
Alert when RAM or swap exceeds threshold
